author: @ThisRandomUsername
text: Glad it doesn't seem like there's lasting damage. One thing is I don't see any way you're getting better performance by having a more pressurised cooling system. I wouldn't expect there to be a pressure sensor for the ECU to read.

author: @dr_ned_flanders
text: Don't you need to let out the excess air after you put the coolant in otherwise there could be air  in the cooling system? i.e. leave it running with the radiator cap off.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
The overflow tank on modern vehicles eliminates that problem.

All you need to do is just keep checking to make sure it's at the correct level after a day of driiving.

replies.author: @dexterconnolly3696
replies.text: Scott tovey some modern cars yes but if you own a turbo subaru and rely on faith that assumption can be game over

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@dexterconnolly3696  @Dexter Connolly  

Now that you mentioned it; I do recall a scenario in which a cooling system will retain air, and that is if the vehicle is on an incline.

As far as the subaru is concerned, that tells me that the engineers did not take getting air out of the system into account. It shouldn't be all that hard to design an ICE with all it's coolant channels having a slight incline when the vehicle is on level ground.

In a liquid, air always travels up; so if the coolant channels are designed in this way, air will naturally travel towards the radiator.
